task: I’m thinking about buying a used car soon, but I want to avoid tying up too much cash or ending up with a money pit. Can you help me narrow down what to prioritize and what to skip so I don’t regret it later?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — prioritize **low total cost of ownership**, not just the sticker price.

### Prioritize
- **Reliable engine/transmission combo**: avoid “interesting” powertrains unless you know the exact model/year is solid.
- **Service history**: regular oil changes, major maintenance done, no skipped intervals.
- **Clean title + no accident history**: especially avoid structural damage.
- **Reasonable mileage for the age**: not just low miles; look for consistent use and maintenance.
- **Inspection results**: pay for a pre-purchase inspection if you can.
- **Known parts availability**: common models are cheaper to fix.
- **Insurance and fuel costs**: a cheap car can still be expensive to own.

### Skip or be cautious with
- **Modified cars**: lowered, tuned, aftermarket turbo, loud exhaust, etc.
- **“Too good to be true” prices**: often hiding damage, deferred maintenance, or title issues.
- **Luxury brands with cheap purchase prices**: repairs can be brutal.
- **Cars with incomplete records**: especially if the seller can’t explain gaps.
- **Flood, salvage, or rebuilt titles**: usually not worth the risk.
- **Models with known expensive failures**: CVT issues, timing chain problems, oil burning, etc.
- **Anything that needs “just a little work”** if you want to avoid a money pit.

### Best strategy
1. Set a **hard all-in budget**: purchase + tax + registration + inspection + immediate repairs.
2. Leave a **repair reserve** after buying.
3. Focus on **boring, common, well-documented cars**.
4. Get a **pre-purchase inspection** before you commit.
5. Assume every used car needs some money right away.

If you want, I can help you build a **used-car filter** based on:
- your budget
- how long you want to keep it
- manual vs automatic
- sedan/SUV/truck
- your tolerance for repairs

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. Too broad though.

What I actually want is a filter that keeps me out of trouble fast:
- max out-the-door budget
- age/mileage cutoff
- brands/models to lean toward
- brands/models to avoid
- what inspection findings are automatic no

I don’t need a lecture on “reliable engine/transmission combo.” I know the concept. Give me a tighter short list, ideally for something I can buy without bleeding cash on repairs.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter **fast filter** for a used car that won’t soak up cash.

## 1) Hard budget rule
Set your **max out-the-door budget** as:

**purchase price + tax/title/fees + pre-purchase inspection + immediate repairs + first-year buffer**

If you want to avoid regret, don’t spend all of it on the car itself.

### Practical split
- **80–85%**: car price + fees
- **5–10%**: inspection + registration
- **10–15%**: repair reserve

If the car only works when you use every dollar, skip it.

---

## 2) Age / mileage cutoff
A simple filter that keeps you out of trouble:

- **Best zone:** **5–10 years old**
- **Mileage target:** **under ~100k miles**
- **Upper limit:** **120k miles only if** it has strong records and a clean inspection

### Skip if:
- **15+ years old** unless you specifically want an older car and can wrench
- **Over 140k miles** if your goal is low repair risk
- **Low miles but poor maintenance** — that’s not a bargain

---

## 3) Brands/models to lean toward
If you want boring and low-drama, start here:

### Good default picks
- **Toyota**: Corolla, Camry, RAV4, Highlander
- **Honda**: Civic, Accord, CR-V, Pilot
- **Mazda**: Mazda3, Mazda6, CX-5
- **Lexus**: ES, RX
- **Acura**: TLX, RDX, MDX
- **Subaru**: only if inspection/history are strong and you accept higher maintenance risk than Toyota/Honda

### Also worth a look
- **Ford Fusion** with the right engine/history
- **Buick** sedans/crossovers if you want comfort and lower prices
- **Chevy Impala / Malibu** only if you inspect carefully and price is right

### Best value play
Usually: **Toyota or Honda with average miles and full records**.  
That’s the safest “buy it and stop thinking about it” lane.

---

## 4) Brands/models to avoid
If the goal is “don’t bleed cash,” I’d be cautious or skip these unless you know the exact year/engine is a known good one:

### Usually avoid for low-risk buying
- **BMW**
- **Mercedes-Benz**
- **Audi**
- **Jaguar**
- **Land Rover / Range Rover**
- **Mini**
- **Volkswagen** if you want the lowest repair hassle
- **Nissan with CVT** if you’re trying to avoid transmission risk
- **Chrysler / Dodge / Jeep** in general if reliability is your top priority

### Also be careful with
- Any **luxury badge** bought cheap
- **Turbocharged** versions when a non-turbo option exists
- **Dual-clutch transmissions** if you want simple ownership
- **Hybrid models** only if battery/charging system history is documented

---

## 5) Automatic no inspection findings
These are the kinds of findings that should usually kill the deal:

### Instant no
- **Flood damage**
- **Salvage or rebuilt title**
- **Frame damage / structural repair**
- **Airbag deployed**
- **Transmission slipping, shuddering, or delayed engagement**
- **Engine knocking, overheating, or coolant/oil mixing**
- **Check engine light with unresolved major codes**
- **Rust on frame / subframe / suspension mounting points**
- **Brake or steering issues that feel unsafe**
- **Evidence of odometer rollback**
- **No title, title mismatch, or seller can’t prove ownership**

### Strong no unless heavily discounted and you accept risk
- Leaks that are more than minor
- Multiple warning lights
- Uneven tire wear from alignment/suspension damage
- Bad paint/body mismatch suggesting prior repair
- Signs of deferred maintenance across multiple systems

---

## 6) Fast buy filter
If you want a simple go/no-go rule:

### Buy if:
- clean title
- private or dealer history is believable
- **5–10 years old**
- **under 100k miles**
- Toyota/Honda/Mazda/Lexus/Acura
- inspection is clean
- no major leaks, warning lights, or rust
- price leaves room for repairs

### Skip if:
- luxury brand
- salvage/rebuilt/flood
- over 120k miles without strong records
- CVT/transmission concerns
- rust, warning lights, or sketchy history
- the seller says “just needs a little work”

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page yes/no shopping filter** you can use while browsing listings.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still too loose.

I want the actual cutoff line, not a shopping sermon.

Give me this in a tighter table or bullets:
- max out-the-door price as a % of my cash, not a generic split
- mileage/age cutoff by category: sedan, small SUV, midsize SUV
- exact models I should look at first
- exact models I should not touch
- inspection items that are immediate no vs. negotiable

And don’t hide behind “depends on year/engine.” I know it depends. I want the short list that cuts the field fast.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the **fast cutoff filter** version.

## 1) Max out-the-door price
Use this rule:

- **Max OTD = 80% of your cash**
- Keep **20% back** for repairs, registration surprises, tires, brakes, insurance gap, etc.

If you have:
- **$10k cash** → max OTD **$8k**
- **$15k cash** → max OTD **$12k**
- **$20k cash** → max OTD **$16k**

If the car only works at **100% of your cash**, skip it.

---

## 2) Age / mileage cutoff by category

| Category | Good target | Hard stop |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Sedan** | 6–10 years old, **under 90k miles** | **Over 120k** unless it’s unusually clean and cheap |
| **Small SUV** | 5–9 years old, **under 85k miles** | **Over 110k** |
| **Midsize SUV** | 4–8 years old, **under 75k miles** | **Over 100k** |

### Simple rule
- **Under target** = green light to keep looking
- **At cutoff** = only if price is clearly below market and inspection is clean
- **Over hard stop** = pass

---

## 3) Exact models to look at first

### Sedans
- **Toyota Corolla**
- **Toyota Camry**
- **Honda Civic**
- **Honda Accord**
- **Mazda3**
- **Lexus ES**

### Small SUVs
- **Toyota RAV4**
- **Honda CR-V**
- **Mazda CX-5**
- **Subaru Forester** only if records are strong and inspection is clean

### Midsize SUVs
- **Toyota Highlander**
- **Honda Pilot**
- **Lexus RX**
- **Mazda CX-9** only if priced right and well maintained

If you want the safest short list, start with:
**Corolla, Camry, Civic, Accord, RAV4, CR-V, CX-5, ES, RX**

---

## 4) Exact models not to touch

### Avoid
- **Nissan Rogue**
- **Nissan Altima**
- **Nissan Sentra**
- **Jeep Cherokee**
- **Jeep Compass**
- **Jeep Grand Cherokee** unless you really know the exact vehicle
- **Chrysler 200**
- **Dodge Journey**
- **Fiat 500**
- **Mini Cooper**
- **Range Rover / Land Rover**
- **BMW 3/5 Series**
- **Mercedes C/E Class**
- **Audi A4/Q5**

### Also skip if your goal is low cash risk
- **Any luxury badge bought cheap**
- **Any modified car**
- **Any CVT car** unless you specifically know the exact model/year is safe and the price is very good

---

## 5) Inspection findings: immediate no vs negotiable

| Finding | Action |
|---|---|
| Flood damage | **Immediate no** |
| Salvage / rebuilt title | **Immediate no** |
| Frame / unibody damage | **Immediate no** |
| Airbag deployed | **Immediate no** |
| Engine knock / rod knock | **Immediate no** |
| Transmission slipping / shuddering / delayed engagement | **Immediate no** |
| Overheating history | **Immediate no** |
| Coolant-oil mixing / milkshake fluid | **Immediate no** |
| Rust on frame, subframe, suspension mounts | **Immediate no** |
| Odometer mismatch / rollback signs | **Immediate no** |
| Check engine light with major unresolved codes | **Immediate no** |

### Negotiable only if cheap enough
- Minor oil seepage
- Worn brakes/tires
- Small suspension noise
- Cosmetic damage
- One non-critical warning light, if diagnosed and priced out
- Service items due soon, if the price reflects it

### Not negotiable if you want to avoid a money pit
- Multiple warning lights
- Transmission issues
- Rust underneath
- Any sign of flood or structural damage

---

## 6) The short version
If you want the cleanest filter:

**Buy only if:**
- OTD is **≤ 80% of your cash**
- Sedan is **under 90k**, small SUV **under 85k**, midsize SUV **under 75k**
- It’s one of the **Toyota / Honda / Mazda / Lexus** picks above
- Title is clean
- Inspection has **no immediate-no findings**

**Skip if:**
- Nissan / Jeep / Chrysler / Dodge / luxury German brands
- Over the mileage cutoff
- Any structural, flood, transmission, or overheating issue

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-screen yes/no checklist** you can use while browsing listings.

user_simulator.text: Yeah. That’s usable. I can work with that cutoff.
